coredata.dc:title: Scalable synthesis of highly photoluminescence carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mai, Xuan-Dung
Thi Kim Chi, Tran
Nguyen, Truong-Chung
Ta, Van-Thao
coredata.dc:description: Scaling up the synthesis carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is highly demanded for the deployment of CQDs in diverse applications. Herein, we used glycerol (Gly) as high boiling point solvent that enables scalable synthesis of CQDs from mixtures of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) within few minutes by either thermal heating or microwave. Purified CQDs were spherical with a diameter varying from 2 to 8 nm. The CQDs exhibited a resolved UV absorption band at about 346 nm and a broad photoluminescence (PL) spectrum with a PL quantum yield of 49.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Large-scale
Rapid
Synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–Vis spectra of reaction solution taken at different reaction time; b) The variation of UV absorbance at the characteristic peak of fluorophore with the reaction temperature; c) Thermal analyses of CA-EDA mixture.
a) TEM, b) HR-TEM images and c) Size distribution of CQDs.
a) FTIR, b) Raman, c) XPS survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of d) C 1 s, e) O 1 s f) N 1 s of CQDs.
a) Structure model of CQDs; b) Optical characterizations of CQDs and c) The calculations of PL quantum yield of CQDs.

head.ce:title: Scalable synthesis of highly photoluminescence carbon quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Hanoi Pedagogical University, No. 2, Phuc Yen, Viet Nam.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scaling up the synthesis carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is highly demanded for the deployment of CQDs in diverse applications. Herein, we used glycerol (Gly) as high boiling point solvent that enables scalable synthesis of CQDs from mixtures of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) within few minutes by either thermal heating or microwave. Purified CQDs were spherical with a diameter varying from 2 to 8 nm. The CQDs exhibited a resolved UV absorption band at about 346 nm and a broad photoluminescence (PL) spectrum with a PL quantum yield of 49.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs including graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots and carbon polymer dots [1] have been extensively studied since the first report in 2004 by Xu et al [2]. Due to their visible photoluminescence, biocompatibility and ease of surface functionalization [3,4] CQDs have emerged as potentials materials for biomedical applications [5,6]. Although CQDs could be synthesized by numerous methods using a wide range of carbon resources hydrothermal treatment of CA and EDA are preferred to prepare water-soluble and highly photoluminescence CQDs [3,7,8] (SI, Table S1). Unfortunately, hydrothermal synthesis is difficult to scale up by increasing either reaction volume or substances concentration because the temperature and concentration profiles that are key factor for the nucleation and growth of nanocrystals will significantly change [9,10]. This letter reports the use of Gly as a high-boiling point solvent that allows scaling up the synthesis of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and purification of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Chemicals including CA, EDA and Gly were purchased from Aladdin chemicals. A mixture solution of CA (5 M) and EDA in Gly with a molar ratio CA/ED of 0.5 was placed in a microwave oven (Sunhouse, SHD4823) operating 750 W for 7 min continuously. After the reaction, CQDs were precipitated by adding mixture of acetone and ethanol (8:2 by volume) and collected by centrifugation. CQDs were dispersed in water, re-precipitated with acetone and collected by the centrifugation at 5 °C (10000 rpm, 10 min). This cycle was repeated three times prior to dialysis steps which were carried out by dialyzing sequentially through 2 k Da and 20 k Da.

Absorption spectra of CQDs dissolved in water were conducted on an UV-2450 (Shimadzu) spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQD solutions were carried out on a Nanolog spectrometer. A Jasco FT/IR6300 and a LabRAM HR (Horiba Jobin Yvon) spectrometer were used to conduct FTIR and Raman spectra of CQDs, respectively. X-ray diffraction pattern of solid CQDs was conducted on a D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on an ULVAC PHI 500 (Versa Probe II) using an Al Kα X-ray source. TEM images of CQDs were obtained on a JEM 2100 microscope operating at 200 kV using LaB6 filament.

The formation of CQDs from CA and EDA under hydrothermal conditions has been discussed previously [11,12]. Briefly, intramolecular dehydration condensation forms intermediate fluorophores such as imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo- (IPCA) [11]. IPCA involves in carbonization processes resulting in CQDs containing IPCA on the surfaces [13,14]. In current study, the dehydration and carbonization processes were enabled by heating CA and EDA in Gly under ambient pressure. Apparently, the reaction turned black (Fig. S1) and significant amount of CQDs were formed after 7 min (Fig. S2). The formation of IPCA was suggested by the appearance of an its characteristic absorption peak at about 346 nm as seen in Fig. 1 a [4,11]. Notably, the peak was red-shifted to 355 nm and an absorption tail appeared in 400–500 nm range when increasing the reaction time owing to chemical bonding between IPCA and C sp2 domains that extends the conjugation system (Fig. S3) [12]. Fig. 1b indicates that the concentration of IPCA increased abruptly at about 170 °C and then decreased when the temperature was over 215 °C. There temperatures were in line with thermal analysis on a mixture of CA and EDA shown in Fig. 1c. DTG curve exhibits two prominent weight losing points at about 160 °C and 212 °C at which two endothermic processes were clearly seen in DSC curve. Likely, IPCA was formed at about 160° and took part in carbonization processes at 212 °C.

TEM image in Fig. 2 a reveals CQDs to be spherical with a diameter ranging from 2 to 8 nm (Fig. 2c), which was slightly larger than hydrothermally synthesized CQDs [7]. HR-TEM images of CQDs shown in Fig. 2b exhibits lattice spacing of 3.3 Å which corresponds to (0 0 2) plane of graphite [3,15]. From FTIR spectrum in Fig. 3 a, some important features are noted. A broad absorption at 3280 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H bonds; two bands at 2937 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1 were due to stretching vibration of aliphatic C–H bonds; two peaks at 1647 cm−1 and 1556 cm−1 could be assigned respectively to the vibrations of N–H and C = O bonds in amide [7]. Raman spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 3b) exhibits D band and G band of C sp2 systems at 1330 cm−1 and 1545 cm−1, respectively. However, high D/G intensity ratio and a broad XRD peak at 22° (Fig. S4) imply that CQDs consisted of highly disordered C sp2 systems. CQDs contained C, O and N elements as suggested from XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3b). High-resolution XPS analyses of C, O, and N 1 s shown in Fig. 3d-f suggest these elements were in the forms of graphitic or aliphatic (C = C, C–C), oxygenated (C-O, C = O), pyridinic and pyrrolic (C-N) structures [7,12].

We proposed a structural model for CQDs as cartooned in Fig. 4 a. The optical properties of CQDs are summarized in Fig. 4b. UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibits two peaks at 238 nm and 346 nm which could be assigned to π-π* electronic transitions of C sp2 domains in the carbogenic core and the surface IPCA, respectively [3,4,14]. The position of PL maximum and PL intensity of CQDs varied with excitation wavelength (Fig. S5). CQDs gave the highest PL when excited at about 360 nm, at which PL maximized at about 480 nm. Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra observed at different positions as shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. S6 exhibit a similar broad band at about 360 nm, which resembles to the absorption band of IPCA mentioned above. The results imply that the emission of CQDs was dominated by IPCA. The dominations of IPCA would blur the size-dependences that have been observed in graphene QDs [16]. Relative PL quantum yield (ɸ) of CQDs at 355 nm excitation was determined by comparing with that of quinine sulfate as shown in Fig. 4c to be 49.9%, which is comparable to hydrothermally-synthesized CQDs [11,17,18]. The emission efficiency of CQDs decreased gradually with UV (365 nm) exposure, retained about 60% of the initial value after 10 h (Fig. S7).

The scalable synthesis of CQDs has been demonstrated; over 10 g of CQDs could be obtained by heating CA and EDA in Gly. CQDs were spherical, contained C sp2 domains in the core and IPCA on the surfaces. The optical properties of CQDs were largely dominated by the surface IPCA that was an intermediate product formed at about 160 °C and retained on the CQDs surfaces after carbonization processes. The method demonstrated here in would pave a new way to deploy CQDs in diverse applications.

Xuan-Dung Ma:Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Software. Tran Thi Kim Chi: Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Truong-Chung Nguyen: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Van-Thao Ta: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Vietnam Ministry of Education & Training, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2, and Chemedic Company under grant number B.2018-SP2-13 Vietnam Ministry of Education & Training, Vietnam; Hanoi Pedagogical University 2, Vietnam and Chemedic Company, Vietnam under grant number B.2018-SP2-13.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.127595.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
